All right, so today, we move into our next topic, and it's the motor control section, so we talked about getting all the information into the brain, and how we can construct, you know, take very phenomenal building blocks, build them up to recognize objects, which I said is really kind of almost like a memory process, but again, the question people always ask for is, you know, why do we have such big brains, and the answer, you know, isn't really to learn all that information, but it's to do something with that information, so I think people make a very a very compelling argument that really a big brain actually just allows you to interact in the world in both a coordinated way, an exploratory way to manipulate the world, and with great flexibility.
I'd say that's the two big things about having a cerebral cortex are, again, sophisticated movement and flexibility.
We'll be talking about flexibility later in the class. So obviously these are the kinds of activities that no other creatures do anything sort of like this.

For the motor section, we'll actually first talk about the anatomy, and then we'll talk about some of the disorders of the motor system, and then we'll talk more about the physiology over the course of the semester, over the course of the next three lectures.
So just very briefly about the anatomy, of course, we have a big brain that's gonna control our movements.

We've talked about the motor cortex and so forth. You had the reading article about the hand representation, before and after amputation, but a lot of creatures don't have a big brain up there, and they don't even have a subcortex.
They might just have a spinal cord, and a spinal cord is actually gonna be very important, controls lots of aspects of movement.
And so the most basic unit of the motor system is what we call the stretch reflex.
And that's this notion here this is where you get tested at the doctors when they wrap your knee.
That's gonna send through the alpha motor neuron, or through the dorsal root here, through the sensory neuron, it's gonna send a signal that, hey, the limb has been stretched.
And the body, at least much of the spinal cord, is important for maintaining our posture, keeping us upright.
And so if there's any sort of detected stretch, there's this automatic reflex that says, let me counteract that perturbation.
Something got changed, I want stability in the world. And that's what the stretch reflex is all about.
We have that sensory signal coming in through the back of the spinal cord, and we have a motor neuron coming out from the spinal cord.

And the neurotransduction here then is that that neuron, that alpha motor neuron, is actually gonna synapse, not on another neuron, but it's gonna synapse on the muscle spindles, and it's gonna make those muscle spindles contract, and that's what's gonna produce movement.
So that's the most elementary type of movement we can produce, is a stretch reflex.
It's a single monosynaptic reflex there. One synapse in the spinal cord produces something, some action that's gonna counteract the sensory input.

Of course, what we really don't have is we have a bunch of other stuff up here in the brain that's gonna be able to modulate those spinal reflexes, or modulate the output of the alpha motor neurons to produce coordinated movement.

But I just wanna make this point here. These were some classic experiments at the start of the 20th century, so back in like 1905, where they asked, well, what happens, and it's a very kind of cruel-sounding experiment, but it was a basic science of type here.
What happens if we actually dissect the spinal cord, or make a cut in the spinal cord?
And we know that in human behavior, right, that if you have a cut in the spinal cord down here, you're not gonna be able to walk, right?
If you break your spinal cord, you're unable to walk.
They asked the same question to the cat, and certainly, if you did the same experiment to the cat, its lower legs would crumble.
But what they did is they actually put it on this treadmill here.
They supported the cat so that it could stay upright, and then they started the treadmill.

So the cat has no connection between the brain and the back legs of its body, and yet, as soon as they gave it the stimulus of this moving floor, the cat started walking in a normal manner, sped up the treadmill, and it walked faster, slowed down the treadmill, and it slowed down here.

So again, this is making the point that much of our movement capability, or at least there's a great deal of movement capability that's kind of, in a sense, controlled at the spinal level, at least some very fundamental movement patterns, such as those that are important for locomotion, that we actually don't need to have the brain signals to have that happen.

And indeed, people have shown that with babies, as soon as they're born, practically, they don't move, they can't make any coordinated movement, I mean, they move about and stuff, they fidget, but they don't seem like they're capable of any coordinated movement.

But again, if you do a similar experiment, take a young infant, before they can walk, put them in the water so that their body is supported, then they also will produce the walking type of movement there.
So again, fundamental movement patterns seem to be locked in or have evolved into being controlled at the spinal cord.
Well, if we move up, though, from the spinal cord, we're gonna hit the subcortex.
That's all those structures shown here that lie underneath the cerebral cortex, the most prominent one being the cerebellum back here, and also this complex called the basal ganglia.
We'll actually talk about the basal ganglia in detail next Wednesday.

These parts of the brain, or some of them, are sending fibers right down the spinal cord, so they're gonna synapse on those alpha motor neurons, and so these subcortical structures are capable of producing movement.

We actually call those the extrapyramidal tract. extra-pyramidal track. I'll tell you why by contrast in a minute here, but this is just basically showing that there's extra-pyramidal neurons starting from the red nucleus, the vestibular, and so forth.

Don't have to remember all the details there, just the notion that there's one way to actually regulate the activity of the spinal cord is through these extra-pyramidal pathways that are coming from the sub-cortex.
And then, of course, we have the output coming from the cerebral cortex itself.
And most of it is coming from the primary motor cortex.

And it's coming down what's called the pyramidal track. So again, this is basically referring to the pyramidal track where most of the cell bodies originate in, say, primary motor cortex, or M1, whatever terminology you're using.
There are some descending fibers from the premotor cortex, tissue in front of the motor cortex, or also even from the parietal lobe.
But most of it's coming from the primary motor cortex.
And again, as I said earlier, these are the longest axons in the nervous system.

They're basically going directly from the cortex and having their first synapse down at some level of the spinal cord, some of it atop to control arm movements, some of it down low to control leg movements, and much of it also helping regulate postural control.
So that's the pyramidal track. And these, by contrast, are called the extrapyramidal pathway.
So again, it's really this hierarchical control where the spinal cord and subcortex can help modulate these basic reflexes.
But it's really the cerebral cortex that's going to allow for the fine, coordinated, complex movements that we're capable of producing.
And this figure is just highlighting that at least, again, we talk about the vision.
People will talk about, oh, 50% of the brain is devoted to perception.
Well, the motor folks have the same sort of chauvinism.
They're going to claim that 50% or 70% of the brain is really involved in the control of movement.
So here's the primary motor cortex. Obviously, as we move, we're getting sensory feedback here.
That's going to be very important for regulating our movements.
But as we'll see, there's also some involvement of these parietal regions.
We talked about that the other day, about the dorsal pathway really being vision for action.
And the prefrontal cortex that we can talk about planning.
But again, all that planning that's going on is really usually about what's the kind of action we want to take.
And as the actions become more complicated, we'll see that you actually engage more and more of the prefrontal regions of the cortex.
So in terms of the motor cortex, it's really a fairly recent adaptation, or at least the direct pathway going from the cortex down to the spinal cord.

Now, what about if we get to the motor cortex?
Here, again, was our quadriplegia, our paraplegia. The term we use for talking about a disorder of the motor cortex is hemiplegia.
Again, I should have said plegia means a loss of movement control.

The word hemi means that it's one-sided. That's what you'd expect if you have damage, say a stroke or a tumor, in the motor areas of the cortex, is that the damage is going to be restricted to the contralateral side of the body, and that's where you have the hemiplegia.
If you remember, there's that homunculus there. Where the stroke is will result in what kind of effect you have.
If it happens to be on the side of the motor cortex, then the person's probably going to be hemiplegic in using their limb.

And again, if you walk around, because it's a fairly common disorder, you'll notice people aren't using one of their limbs, and it might be slightly more contracted like this is a very common thing for a person who's had a stroke that's produced...
produced upper limb hemiplegia. Some individuals, though, might have a hemiplegia that's both the upper limb and the lower limb.
Now if it's complete paralysis, that's where you use the word hemiplegia, but in most cases there's some functional recovery, and that's what we call hemiparesis.
Hemiparesis meaning it's a mild version, they have a weakness in the limb rather than a complete loss of the ability to use the limb.

Now, of course, because this is such a devastating thing to many people, you lose a lot of your functionality if you're unable to use one of your limbs, there's a huge amount of research asking about how can we restore function in individuals who've had, say, some sort of cortical damage that produces a hemiparesis.
One big debate has been is the recovery really about a reorganization in the damaged hemisphere, or is it about the recruitment of the intact hemisphere?

So if I ever had a stroke in the right side of the brain, right, and I have difficulty using my left hand, two pathways you might consider for recovery is that maybe things get reorganized in the right hemisphere to recover the functionality, or maybe that the other hemisphere is recruited, it seems to be that it's really much more about what sort of function is still, what residual function remains in the damaged hemisphere.
It seems to be much more important for recovery than actually having some sort of ipsilateral control.

And there's lots of interventions that have been tried out there, restraint-induced therapies where, again, if I've had a stroke in the right side and can't use my left hand, what they'll do is they will restrain the person's good hand and force them to use the weak hand.

There's these robot-assisted movements out there. There's lots of work going on with non-invasive brain stimulation to see if they could facilitate recovery by giving different types, either TMS or TES and so forth.
Here's a recent one. I don't think it's got great, you know, generality.
I think it would be very difficult to, like, get lots of people into treatment with it.
But they basically want to ask, could we improve recovery by allowing a person to monitor the activity in their motor cortex?
So there's some tissue, residual tissue, in the motor cortex, and they're just going to use what's called biofeedback, and they're going to do real-time fMRI.
They're just going to plot what's the bold response. Let's say this is the affected hemisphere, so this person had a left-sided stroke.
They're going to just do in real time the person's told, try to make a movement that's going to activate your left hemisphere motor cortex.
We're going to show you this bar. This bar is going to indicate how strong is the activity in that left hemisphere.
Well, when people have a stroke, they tend to activate both hemispheres, okay?
And so the one line here, one bar here is going to show what's the activity level in the damaged hemisphere.
The other bar is going to show what's the activity in the intact hemisphere.
Again, the game people try to play is, can I make the damaged hemisphere more active than the undamaged hemisphere?

So that's what I mean by they're using biofeedback here, by taking the fMRI signal, projecting it to the person, and they're just sitting there trying to make the movements, getting this continuous feedback, and here are the results showing this, that here they're doing three different runs, and one condition they actually have the real biofeedback, and for a control condition, they actually just randomize what's the level of the bars, not tied to the actual bold response in the damaged hemisphere.
So here they're showing that there actually is some improvement, or at least some degree of control over this one hour session in the person's ability to activate the damaged hemisphere.
More recently, people have also been moving towards invasive stimulation techniques, and this is a fairly mild stimulation technique about just activating the vagus nerve.

The vagus nerve is kind of part of the parasympathetic system that I think I mentioned earlier on, serves almost as an arousal function, and the question is, can you actually just sort of by putting the state in aroused state, can that also facilitate recovery from stroke?
And this is just sort of the data that's come out from this recent study.
At baseline, they're sort of measuring this Fuglmeier, is a scale that's indicating how well you can control your upper limbs.
So a low score is poorly, a high score is good.
They're showing that if they do rehabilitation, say for six weeks, this is the degree of achievement they're able to achieve.
About two points on that scale, and basically to the patient, that wouldn't be noticeable.
They're getting a bit better, but it's not something that's going to have an impact on their everyday life.
But if they do the rehabilitation in combination with the stimulation, now they're able to get about a six point improvement, and that actually does translate into some improved functionality.
So lots of excitement in the world about how can we use either non-invasive or invasive methods to help recovery.

about actually on Monday is some really radical things about invasive brain stimulation and how that's being used to help some of these people have these hemiplegias or these severe movement disorders.

All right, so then moving up further up the system, so now first we talked about damage to the spinal cord, then the subcortex, then the primary motor cortex, but now what about those secondary motor areas?
I mentioned that the parietal lobe seems to be associated with movement, as does the premotor cortex or the prefrontal cortex, and that is what produces a condition called apraxia.
And apraxia, like many different types of neurological syndromes, is almost defined by exclusion.
These are individuals who have trouble with producing coordinated movement, but it's not because of any problem in the loss of control of the muscles.
So with the ataxia patient we saw, the cerebellar ataxia, they have kind of lost control of the muscles.
They couldn't make a smooth coordinated movement. The person with the motor cortex lesion, they have weakness.
They can't recruit those motor units. They can't recruit, send signals down to activate the spinal cord.
The people with apraxia, they have the normal strength, they have normal coordination.
The movements they produce are smooth for the most part, but what they can't do is come up with sort of the knowledge or connect up that knowledge about what you want to do with an action.
